title: Charging, Conductors & Insulators
targetIntuitions: Conductors (metals) have free electrons that roam; insulators hold their electrons locked to atoms
A charged object polarizes a nearby neutral object, and because the near side is the opposite charge and closer, the net force is attraction
Charging by conduction (touch) leaves the same sign; charging by induction (no touch, using a ground) leaves the opposite sign
Grounding connects an object to the earth, a limitless reservoir, so electrons flow in or out freely
Insulators polarize too, molecule by molecule, with no free flow
Excess charge on a conductor spreads out and rides the outer surface
learningArc: The sticky balloon puzzle, then conductors vs insulators, then polarization, then why neutral objects attract, then conduction (same sign), then grounding, then induction (opposite sign), then insulator polarization, then charge on the surface, then a summary that closes the puzzle

steps.title: The sticky balloon

visual.description: A charged balloon clinging flat against a wall, with the wall labeled neutral.

steps.body: Rub a balloon on your hair and it sticks to the wall. You already know the balloon picks up charge by trading electrons. But the wall is neutral, with just as many plus as minus charges and no leftover charge at all. So how can a neutral wall pull on anything? The answer is about how charge moves around inside materials, which is what this lesson is about.

steps.title: Where can electrons move?

visual.description: A metal slab and a plastic slab, each dotted with electrons. Tapping nudges them: they glide in the metal and barely budge in the plastic.

steps.prompt: Tap each material to nudge its electrons. In which one can the electrons move freely?

feedback.correct: Right. In the metal the electrons slide across the whole slab. In the plastic they stay pinned in place.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: Tap the plastic again and watch closely. Its electrons barely move. It is the metal whose electrons glide freely.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: Compare the two. The metal's electrons travel across it, while the plastic's stay put. They are not the same.

steps.explanation: In a metal the electrons roam across the whole slab; in a plastic they stay locked to their atoms. That single difference drives everything else in this lesson.

steps.title: Two kinds of material

visual.description: Side by side: a copper bar labeled conductor and a rubber block labeled insulator.

steps.body: You just saw the difference. Materials fall into two camps. In a conductor, like any metal, charge can move through the material freely. In an insulator, like rubber, glass, or plastic, charge is stuck where it is and barely moves at all. Same charges as always, plus and minus. The only new idea is whether those charges are free to travel.

steps.title: The sea of free electrons

visual.description: A metal lattice of fixed positive cores with a shared cloud of electrons drifting between them.

steps.body: Inside a metal, each atom gives up an outer electron or two. Those electrons leave their atoms and join a shared sea that drifts through the whole piece of metal, while the heavy positive cores stay fixed. That free sea is what lets a conductor carry charge from place to place. An insulator has no such sea: every electron stays bound to its own atom.

steps.title: Conductor or insulator?

visual.description: Three labeled objects to choose from: a copper wire, a rubber balloon, and a glass rod.

steps.prompt: Which of these is the conductor?

feedback.correct: Yes. Copper is a metal, so it has the free electron sea and conducts.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: Rubber is an insulator. Its electrons are locked to their atoms, which is exactly why a rubbed balloon keeps its charge instead of leaking it away.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: Glass is an insulator, not a conductor. Look for the metal in the list.

steps.explanation: Metals conduct because of their free electrons. Rubber, glass, and most plastics are insulators.

steps.title: Charge nearby disturbs it

visual.description: A neutral metal sphere with a charged rod approaching from one side, the sphere's electron sea starting to shift.

steps.body: Now bring a charged rod close to a neutral piece of metal, without touching it. The metal stays neutral overall, but its free electrons feel the rod's force. Remember from Coulomb's Law that like charges repel and opposite charges attract. Those same forces are about to push the free sea around inside the metal.

steps.title: Polarize the metal

visual.description: A draggable negative rod and a neutral metal sphere. As the rod nears, the sphere's electrons slide to the far side, leaving the near side positive.

steps.prompt: Drag the negative rod toward the metal sphere. Where do the sphere's free electrons go?

choices.text: To the far side, away from the rod
To the near side, toward the rod

feedback.correct: Right. The negative rod repels the sphere's electrons, pushing them to the far side and leaving the near side positive.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: The rod is negative and so are the electrons. Like charges repel, so the electrons are pushed away from the rod, not toward it.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: Watch the sphere as the rod gets close. The free electrons do move. Only an insulator's charges would stay put.

steps.explanation: This is polarization: a nearby charge separates a conductor's charge, electrons to one side and positive cores left on the other, even though the sphere is still neutral overall.

steps.title: Which side turns positive?

visual.description: The polarized sphere with the negative rod on the left; the near and far halves are tappable.

steps.prompt: The negative rod is on the left. Tap the side of the sphere that becomes positive.

choices.text: The near side, closest to the rod
The far side, away from the rod

feedback.correct: Yes. The electrons fled to the far side, so the near side is left short of electrons, which makes it positive.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: The far side is where the electrons piled up, so it is negative. The side they left behind, the near side, is the positive one.

steps.explanation: Wherever the electrons leave is positive; wherever they gather is negative. The near side, facing the negative rod, ends up positive.

steps.title: Why neutral things attract

visual.description: The polarized sphere showing a large attraction arrow from the near side and a small repulsion arrow from the far side, summing to a net pull toward the rod.

steps.body: Here is the payoff. The near side is now the opposite charge to the rod, so it is pulled toward the rod. The far side is the same charge, so it is pushed away. But the near side is closer, and from Coulomb's Law force grows fast as distance shrinks, by one over r squared. So the near attraction beats the far repulsion, and the neutral object is pulled in. That is why a charged thing attracts neutral ones.

steps.title: See the net pull

visual.description: Drag the rod; the near attraction arrow and far repulsion arrow update live, and a net arrow shows the total force on the sphere.

steps.prompt: Drag the rod and watch the two forces on the sphere's separated charge. Which way is the net force?

feedback.correct: Right. The near attraction is stronger than the far repulsion, so the net force points toward the rod.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: Look at the arrow lengths. The near attraction arrow is longer than the far repulsion arrow, so the total points toward the rod.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: They would cancel only if both sides were the same distance away. The near side is closer, so by one over r squared its pull wins.

steps.explanation: Equal amounts of separated charge do not give equal forces, because the near charge is closer. Inverse square makes the near attraction win, so the net force is always a pull.

steps.title: Attract or repel?

visual.description: A charged rod held near a neutral metal can, with a question mark over the can.

steps.prompt: A charged rod is held near a neutral metal can, for either sign of rod. What does the can do?

choices.text: It is attracted to the rod
It is repelled by the rod

feedback.correct: Yes. Whatever the rod's sign, polarization always puts the opposite charge nearer, so the can is always attracted.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: The can rearranges so the opposite charge faces the rod. Opposite charges attract, so the can is pulled in, not pushed away.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: Neutral does not mean unaffected. The rod separates the can's charge, and the near opposite charge wins, so the can is attracted.

steps.explanation: A charged object attracts a neutral conductor regardless of the charge's sign, because the conductor always polarizes with the opposite charge closer.

steps.title: Three ways to charge

visual.description: Three small panels: friction (rubbing), conduction (touching), induction (a rod held near plus a ground wire).

steps.body: There are three ways to give an object a net charge. Friction you already met with the balloon: rubbing trades electrons between two materials. The two new ones are conduction, charging by touching, and induction, charging without touching by using a nearby charge and a ground. Let's build up each one.

steps.title: Charging by conduction

visual.description: A negative rod touches a neutral metal sphere; electrons spread from the rod onto the sphere; the rod is pulled away leaving the sphere negative.

steps.prompt: Touch the negative rod to the metal sphere, then pull it away. What charge is left on the sphere?

choices.text: Negative, the same sign as the rod

feedback.correct: Right. On contact, some of the rod's extra electrons spread onto the sphere and stay there, leaving it negative.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: The rod has extra electrons and they spread onto the sphere. Gaining electrons makes the sphere negative, the same sign as the rod, not the opposite.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: Touching lets charge cross from the rod to the sphere. After contact the sphere keeps some of that charge, so it is no longer neutral.

steps.explanation: Conduction shares charge through contact. The charge that crosses over has the same sign as the charging object, so the sphere ends up negative like the rod.

steps.title: What sign after touching?

visual.description: A before and after pair: a charged object touches a neutral one; both end the same sign.

steps.prompt: Charging by conduction leaves the touched object with which sign?

choices.text: The same sign as the charging object

feedback.correct: Yes. Contact shares charge of the same sign, so both objects end up charged the same way.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: Opposite is induction, which is coming next. Conduction is plain contact, and contact shares the same sign.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: Charge really does transfer on contact, so the object ends up charged, not neutral.

steps.explanation: Conduction means same sign: the charge flows across on contact and both objects share it.

steps.title: Grounding

visual.description: A charged sphere with a wire running down to the earth, electrons flowing along the wire.

steps.body: One more tool before induction: grounding. Connect an object to the earth with a conductor and you join it to a reservoir so huge it never runs out of room for charge. Extra electrons can flow off into the ground, or electrons can flow up out of the ground, whichever the situation calls for. The ground simply soaks up or supplies electrons as needed.

steps.title: Drain it to ground

visual.description: A negatively charged sphere with a grounding wire; tapping connects the ground and the extra electrons flow away to earth.

steps.prompt: This sphere is negatively charged. Connect it to ground. What happens to its charge?

feedback.correct: Right. The extra electrons repel each other and escape down the wire into the earth, leaving the sphere neutral.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: The electrons leave until the sphere has no excess, which is neutral. They do not overshoot and leave it positive.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: Grounding gives the crowded electrons a way out. Watch them flow down the wire; the sphere ends up neutral.

steps.explanation: Grounding a charged conductor lets its excess charge drain away, so it returns to neutral.

steps.title: The induction trick

visual.description: A rod held near a grounded sphere, never touching, with a hint that the sphere will end up oppositely charged.

steps.body: Now combine polarization and grounding into a clever trick: induction. It charges a conductor without ever touching it, and it leaves the opposite sign to the rod you used. The order of moves matters, so watch each one carefully in the next step.

steps.title: Charging by induction

visual.description: A four stage scene advanced by a button: bring a negative rod near (electrons flee to the far side), ground the far side (those electrons leave to earth), remove the ground, then remove the rod, leaving the sphere positive.

steps.prompt: Step through induction with the button. After the rod is gone, what is the sphere's charge?

choices.text: Negative, the same as the rod

feedback.correct: Right. Grounding let the repelled electrons escape, and once the ground and then the rod are gone, the sphere is left with a net positive charge.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: The rod never touches the sphere, so no electrons come from the rod. Instead the rod's repulsion drove electrons out through the ground, leaving the sphere positive.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: It would stay neutral only without the grounding step. Because electrons left to earth while the rod was near, the sphere keeps a net charge after the rod leaves.

steps.explanation: Induction: the rod polarizes the sphere, the ground drains the repelled electrons away, and removing the ground then the rod traps the opposite charge. The sphere ends positive for a negative rod, without contact.

steps.title: What sign after induction?

visual.description: A before and after pair: a rod held near a grounded conductor; the conductor ends with the opposite sign.

steps.prompt: Charging by induction leaves the object with which sign?

choices.text: The opposite sign to the nearby charge
The same sign as the nearby charge

feedback.correct: Yes. Induction always leaves the opposite sign, because you remove the charge the rod repelled.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: Same sign is conduction, which needs contact. Induction works at a distance and leaves the opposite sign.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: The grounding step removes charge while the rod is near, so a net charge is left behind once the rod is gone.

steps.explanation: Induction leaves the opposite sign to the rod, the mirror image of conduction's same sign.

steps.title: Order the induction steps

visual.description: Four shuffled step cards to drag or reorder into the correct induction sequence.

steps.prompt: Put the steps of charging by induction in the right order.

items.label: Bring the charged rod near the sphere

feedback.correct: Exactly. Ground while the rod is still near, disconnect the ground, and only then take the rod away.

wrong.text: The key is the ending: disconnect the ground before you remove the rod. If the rod leaves first, the electrons just flow back from the ground and the sphere returns to neutral.

steps.explanation: Order matters. The rod must stay near while you ground and then unground, so the trapped charge cannot escape. Remove the rod last.

steps.title: Insulators polarize too

visual.description: An insulator block of tiny molecules; near a charged rod each molecule rotates into a small dipole so the opposite charge faces the rod.

steps.body: Insulators have no free sea, so charge cannot flow across them. But they still respond. Each molecule stretches and turns into a tiny dipole, plus on one end and minus on the other, lining up so the opposite charge faces the rod. The surface nearest the rod ends up slightly opposite, just enough to attract. That is the real reason the charged balloon clings to the neutral wall.

steps.title: Polarize an insulator

visual.description: A draggable charged rod near an insulator made of little dipole molecules that rotate to face the rod as it approaches.

steps.prompt: Drag the charged rod near the insulator. What happens inside it?

choices.text: Its molecules rotate so the opposite charge faces the rod
Its electrons flow to the far side

feedback.correct: Right. The bound molecules turn into little dipoles and line up, so the surface facing the rod becomes oppositely charged.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: That is what happens in a conductor. An insulator has no free electrons to flow, so instead its molecules rotate in place.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: Insulators do respond, just not by flowing. Watch the molecules turn so their opposite end faces the rod.

steps.explanation: Insulators polarize molecule by molecule. No charge travels across, but the lined up dipoles leave an opposite charge on the near surface, which is enough to attract.

steps.title: Charge rides the surface

visual.description: A solid metal blob with all its excess electrons spread evenly around the outer surface and none inside.

steps.body: One last fact about conductors. If you add extra charge to a metal, the charges all repel each other and push apart as far as they can. The only way to get as far apart as possible is to spread out over the outer surface, with none left in the interior. On any conductor in balance, the excess charge lives on the surface.

steps.title: Spread to the surface

visual.description: A conductor outline; added electrons are dropped inside and then relax outward, settling spread around the surface.

steps.prompt: Add extra electrons to the metal and let them settle. Where do they end up?

choices.text: Spread out on the outer surface

feedback.correct: Right. They repel each other and settle on the surface, as far apart as they can get.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: They do not stay where you drop them. Each electron pushes the others away, so they keep moving until they reach the surface.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: The center is the most crowded spot, the opposite of what mutual repulsion wants. They spread to the outer surface instead.

steps.explanation: Mutual repulsion drives excess charge to the outer surface of a conductor, leaving the interior with no net charge.

steps.title: Share the charge

visual.description: A metal sphere with charge Q touching an identical neutral sphere, then the two separated.

steps.prompt: A metal sphere carries charge Q. You touch it to an identical neutral sphere, then separate them. How much charge sits on each, as a fraction of Q?

feedback.correct: Right. Two identical conductors share charge equally, so each ends with Q over 2.

wrong.text: The spheres are identical, so the charge splits evenly between them. Divide Q by 2.

steps.explanation: Touching identical conductors lets charge flow until they match, so a charge Q splits into Q over 2 on each.

steps.title: You explained the balloon

visual.description: The opening scene returns: the charged balloon on the wall, now annotated with the wall's molecules polarized to face it.

steps.body: That is the whole story. Conductors have free charge, insulators do not. A charged object polarizes whatever is near, and because the opposite charge ends up closer, neutral things are always attracted. Touching shares the same sign (conduction); the no touch ground trick leaves the opposite sign (induction); grounding drains charge to the earth; and excess charge on a conductor rides the surface. The balloon sticks because it polarizes the wall's molecules, so the wall's surface pulls right back.
